coredata.dc:title: Methyl orange-derived carbon dots for light-emitting-diode and fluorescent electrospun nanofibers
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Yawen
Zhang, Shuai
Li, Shanggeng
Li, Jing
Li, Ning
Zhang, Lin
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have gained tremendous attention over a wide range from fluorescence sensors to optoelectronic devices. In this context, N, S-codoped carbon dots (MO-CDs) of 2.7 nm were synthesized via a facile hydrothermal method using a single precursor Methyl Orange (MO). Upon excitation at 430 nm, the MO-CDs exhibit a yellow emission with an absolute quantum yield (QY) of 15.3%. In addition, the as-prepared MO-CDs possess excellent resistance to high ion strength, high H2O2 concentration, and UV exposure. Because of their good fluorescence properties, a light-emitting diode (LED) device that emits yellow light was fabricated by mixing MO-CDs and siloxane. Moreover, the electrospun nanofibers with yellow fluorescence were prepared by electrospinning technology. This work shows that the CDs derived from MO possess special fluorescent characteristics, which can be utilized as a multifunctional carbon material.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Methyl orange
Light-emitting-diode
Electrospun nanofibers

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Application of the MO-CDs in LED

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM photograph of MO-CDs, inset: HR-TEM image. (b) Particle size distribution histogram of MO-CDs. (c) FTIR spectrum of MO-CDs. (d) Raman spectrum of MO-CDs.
(a) XPS full survey spectrum of MO-CDs. (b–f) The high-resolution XPS spectra and fitted data of C 1s, S 2p, O 1s, N 1s, and Na 1s.

(a) UV–vis absorbance of MO-CDs (black) and MO solution (red). (b) Normalized fluorescence emission (black) and excitation (red) spectra of MO-CDs (inset: photographs of MO-CDs solution under (left) room light and (right) 365 nm UV lamp). (c) 3D fluorescence contour map of MO-CDs solution.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of MO-CDs/siloxane composite (from 360 nm to 480 nm). (b) Photographs of the yellow LED lamp with and without (inset) operating voltage. (c) emission spectrum with the inset of a. (d) color coordinate of the LED.

(a) and (d) SEM images of PAN nanofibers. (b–c) Elemental maps of C, and N for PAN nanofibers. (e) The TEM image of MO-CDs based nanofibers. (f) Confocal fluorescence microscopy photograph of MO-CDs based nanofibers, emission wavelength: 430 nm. Scale bar: a–e:1 μm, f: 25 μm.
(a) TEM photograph of luminescent nanofibers. (b–f) Elemental maps of C, N, Na, O, and S for luminescent nanofibers. Scale bar: 300 nm.
The schematic representation of the synthesis and application of MO-CDs.

head.ce:title: Methyl orange-derived carbon dots for light-emitting-diode and fluorescent electrospun nanofibers

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center of Laser Fusion, China Academy of Engineering Physics, Mianyang, 621900, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Center of Laser Fusion, China Academy of Engineering Physics, Mianyang 621900, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Nuclear Science and Technology, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei, 230026, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Nuclear Science and Technology
University of Science and Technology of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Nuclear Science and Technology, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei 230026, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have gained tremendous attention over a wide range from fluorescence sensors to optoelectronic devices. In this context, N, S-codoped carbon dots (MO-CDs) of 2.7 nm were synthesized via a facile hydrothermal method using a single precursor Methyl Orange (MO). Upon excitation at 430 nm, the MO-CDs exhibit a yellow emission with an absolute quantum yield (QY) of 15.3%. In addition, the as-prepared MO-CDs possess excellent resistance to high ion strength, high H2O2 concentration, and UV exposure. Because of their good fluorescence properties, a light-emitting diode (LED) device that emits yellow light was fabricated by mixing MO-CDs and siloxane. Moreover, the electrospun nanofibers with yellow fluorescence were prepared by electrospinning technology. This work shows that the CDs derived from MO possess special fluorescent characteristics, which can be utilized as a multifunctional carbon material.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: I have shared my data at the attach file step

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as a rising star of luminescent carbon-based nanomaterials, arouse extensive attention owing to their fascinating advantage in unique photoluminescence, high water solubility, and excellent biocompatibility [1–3]. These inherent advantages make CDs different from traditional fluorescent materials; and enable various applications in analyte and detection [4], chemosensing [5], bioimaging [6,7], cancer diagnosis [8], anti-counterfeiting [1], and optoelectronic devices [9,10]. In the above study, the regulation of the optical properties and the applications of CDs have always been a hot topic for researchers. In this regard, CDs that emit at long wavelengths will be more suitable than those that emit at short wavelengths [11]. For light-emitting-diode (LED), the short wavelength such as blue light has a particularly bad effect on human health for instance many diseases of the skin (erythema, photoaging, skin cancer, etc.) and eyes (cataract, myopia, keratopathy, macular degeneration, and maculopathy) [12]. On the other hand, long wavelength emission is good for enhancing the penetration depth and avoiding the interference from background irradiation [13]. To resolve the above problems, long wavelength emissive CDs can be synthesized by controlling the size distribution [14], regulating the oxidation of the surface state [12], optimizing the reaction conditions [15,16], and doping heteroatom [17,18].

Among many improved methods, heteroatom doping is one of the most effective ways to redshift the wavelength of CDs. Up to now, many studies previously reported have shown that heteroatom (for example B, N, P, and S) can effectively provide active sites, enhancing the affinity between CDs and analytes, and further broadening the potential applications [5,19]. Besides, the structure of the precursors is also crucial to the red shift of the emission wavelength of CDs [16]. There are many reports on synthesizing CDs using sugars, small organic molecules, and other precursors. In general, the presence of aromatic ring structure and heteroatom in the precursor, is very favorable for prolonging the emission wavelength CDs. In addition, the onefold precursor is more conducive to improving the uniformity of CDs, such as the size distribution, the consistency of the chemical structure, and the repeatability of the experimental scheme. All these excellent properties make it better than multi-precursor synthesis.

As a kind of azo dyes, Methyl Orange (MO) is widely available, inexpensive, and commonly used in the laboratory for acid-base titration. On the other side, there are two benzene rings, nonmetallic elements such as N, O, and S, and the metallic element Na in the molecular structure of MO, which makes it fascinating as a precursor. Inspired by the previous original work, herein we select it as the only raw material through a one-step hydrothermal method to prepare CDs. The obtained MO-CDs emit bright yellow fluorescence at the excitation at 430 nm. Also, they possess excellent resistance to high ion strength, high H2O2 concentration, and UV exposure. Subsequently, the MO-CDs are utilized to produce LED coatings and light-emitting electrospinning. The synthesis scheme for preparing and applying MO-CDs is presented schematically in Scheme 1 .

Ferric chloride and Mercuric nitrate were got from Sigma. The other chemicals such as Methyl Orange, Polyacrylonitrile (PAN, Mw = 150,000 g/mol), N, N-dimethyl formamide (DMF, 99.5%), and so on were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Co., Ltd. These reagents were used directly without further purified. Ultrapure water used throughout the whole experiment was purified by a Millipore system.

UV–vis absorption spectroscopy was measured with a UV-8000 spectrophotometer (Metash, Shanghai, China) and fluorescence spectroscopy was performed with an F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). The morphology and lateral size of the MO-CDs were observed through a transmission electron microscope (TEM; JEOL-JEM 2100F, Japan). Raman spectrum was collected with a LabRAM−HR Raman spectrometer (Horiba Scientific, Japan). Functional group analysis of MO-CDs was recorded by a Nicolet Is5 spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted on a Thermo Scientific spectrometer (America, USA) equipped with a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source (hν = 1486.6 eV) operating at 100 W. The absolute fluorescence QY was carried out using a fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments FLS1000). The confocal fluorescence microscopy photograph was performed on a Leica TCS-SP8 SR laser scanning confocal microscope.

In a typical preparation of MO-CDs, 50 mg of MO was dissolved directly in a 10 mL mixture of water and methanol (the volume ratio is 1:1) and ultrasound for several minutes to get a homogeneous solution. Then, the obtained solution was put into a 50 mL Teflon-lined reaction still and heated to 180 °C for 5 h. Subsequently, the naturally cooled solution was filtered with a 0.22 μm membrane filter to get rid of the large particles. Furthermore, the reddish-brown solution was subjected to dialysis for 24 h (Mw = 100–500 Da). Finally, the solution was vacuum freeze-dried, and the solid powder was kept at 4 °C for further use.

GaN chips without phosphor covering were bought from Shenzhen Wang Long Technology to prepare LED based on MO-CDs. The chips emission peak was located at 365 nm with the operating voltage of 3 V. Typically, the N-CDs powders were dispersed into siloxane homogeneously and then directly dropped onto the surface of the chip. Eventually, the chip was exposed to UV radiation for 1 min for polymerizing. The optical properties were tested by a spectroradiometer system (HP-8000, China). All measurements were carried out in the darkroom.

Electrospun nanofibers were prepared as shown in Fig. S1. Typically, PAN powders were dissolved in DMF for 3 h under vigorous stirring at 40 °C at concentrations of 10 wt%. Then, this viscous and homogeneous precursor solution was pipetted into a 10 mL plastic syringe with a blunt-ended needle of 0.8 mm diameter. All the nanofibers were electrospun prepared at a high voltage of 10 kV, and the flow rate of the pump was set with 0.5 mL/h. The electrode distance between the needle and the ground collector wrapped in aluminum foil was 10 cm. After evaporation of the solvent, the nanofibers were dried naturally in the air and stored in a desiccator for further use. In the preparation of fluorescent electrospinning (MO-CDs based nanofibers), the concentration of MD-CDs was 5 over ten thousand, and the rest of the preparation process was the same as PAN nanofibers.

In this work, the MO-CDs were prepared by a simple one-step hydrothermal method using MO as the sole precursor (Scheme 1). Many reports demonstrated that heteroatom (metallic ions or nonmetallic atoms) is crucial for the regulation of the emission of CDs redshift [19–21]. Among the heteroatom-doping, especially the S atom has a lower electronegativity and can induce synergistic effects with the N atom [11,22]. On these grounds, MO was selected as a single precursor to prepare yellow CDs by hydrothermal treatment for 5 h at 180 °C.

The morphological and crystal structure of the MO-CDs were analyzed through TEM. As depicted in Fig. 1 a, the MO-CDs are uniformly distributed and spherical in the shape without aggregation. Meanwhile, the MO-CDs exhibit a relatively narrow size-distribution between 2.1 and 3.7 nm with an average diameter of 2.7 nm (Fig. 1b) from the size-distribution analysis of 100 particles. The high-resolution TEM image (inset in Fig. 1a) demonstrates the lattice fringe of MO-CDs is approximately 0.22 nm, which is close to the diffraction plane (100) of graphitic carbon [23]. The Raman spectroscopy also verifies that, with two distinct peaks at 1380 cm−1 and 1590 cm−1, corresponding to the disordered sp3 carbon (D-band) and graphitized sp2 carbon (G-band) in the carbon material [11,24]. The I D/I G ratio is 0.98, indicating the presence of graphene-like structures in the MO-CDs.

Moreover, the surface structure and element composition of MO-CDs have been investigated by FTIR and XPS analyses. As demonstrated in the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1c), the characteristic peak around 3339 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of N–H and O–H groups [25]. Additionally, the absorption peak at 2958 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of C–H [26], and the characteristic absorption peak at 1630 cm−1 is associated with the asymmetric stretching vibration of CN/CO [27]. In addition, a few absorption peaks at 1590 cm−1, 1491 cm−1, 1180 cm−1, 1123 cm−1, 1025 cm−1 correspond to CC, the bending vibration of N–H, C–N, C–S, C–O, and –SO3 -, respectively [9,28].

As shown in Fig. 2 a, the MO-CDs are consisted of C 1s (284.82 eV), N 1s (399.3 eV), O1s (531.78 eV), S 2p (168.38 eV), and Na 1s (1071.4 eV), in the proportions of C: 56.05%, N: 8.04%, O: 26.04%, S: 5.88%, and Na: 3.98%, severally. For one thing, the carbon content of MO-CDs is larger than the raw material, suggesting that MO has undergone some degree of carbonization. For another, the content of oxygen and nitrogen is 34.08% in MO-CDs, indicating their good hydrophilicity [29]. The high-resolution curves of C 1s (Fig. 2 b) display three characteristic peaks of C–C/CC (284.82 eV), C–C/C–S (285.9 eV), and CO (288.4 eV). Also, the fine spectrum of C is dominated by C–C/CC, demonstrating the presence of a good deal of graphitic structures [30]. In Fig. 2c, the characteristic peaks in the high-resolution spectra of S 2p at 167.2 and 168.1 eV belong to S2p1/2 and S2p3/2 [9,28]. As shown in Fig. 2d, the O 1s peak at 530.6 and 531.7 eV corresponds to SO and CO, respectively [15]. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2e) can be decomposed into two characteristic peaks at 398.4, and 401.1 eV, corresponding to pyrrolic N and graphitic N [31], respectively. The peak at 1070.6 eV (Fig. 2f) belongs to the Na 1s spectrum, which manifests that there are metal elements on the surface of the MO-CDs. All the FITR and XPS spectra results reveal an abundance of hydrophilic groups on the surface of MO-CDs, which is conducive to dissolution.

As displayed in Fig. 3 a, the UV–visible spectrum of MO-CDs is completely different from the absorption spectrum of the raw material. It represents a sharp absorption band at 254 nm, which is assigned to the π-π* electronic transition in the aromatic conjugated system [26]. In addition, the spectrum has a broad tail band in the range from 300 nm to 500 nm, corresponding to n-π* edge transition [32]. Especially, this n-π* transition may be related to the heteroatomic non-bonding orbital and the surface state of MO-CDs. As demonstrated in Fig. 3b inset, the solution of MO-CDs is clear and colorless under sunlight irradiation; but emits bright yellow-green fluorescence under 365 nm UV light. The FL spectrum of MO-CDs shows the optimal emission wavelength at 550 nm on the excitation of 430 nm (Fig. 3b). In addition, the obtained MO-CDs embody excitation-independent properties under the excitation wavelength in the range from 350 to 520 nm (Fig. 3c). This may be associated with the uniformity of size distribution and surface chemical states. Moreover, the absolute quantum yield in methol (calculated by integrating sphere) is 15.3%, and the luminescent efficiency is comparable to that of CDs prepared from other dyes [33]. Furthermore, the prepared MO-CDs are stable in the salty, common metal ions solution and oxidizing medium. Besides, they are more resistant to photobleaching than conventional CDs (Fig. S2) [34,35]. Nevertheless, it was found that the fluorescence intensity changed greatly in the pH range from 1 to 7, and was almost constant in the alkaline range. This result is consistent with the Zeta potential changed from 3.56 mV to −41.9 mV (Table S1), indicating that the system has undergone an equilibrium of protonation and deprotonation [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the MO-CDs in LED
ce:section.ce:para: Ascribe to the particular optical properties and excellent stability of MO-CDs, we fabricated a warm LED device by mixing it with siloxane and dropped directly onto the chip of the emission at 365 nm. As illustrated in Fig. 4 a, the MO-CDs/siloxane composite exhibits a similar fluorescence spectrum to the solution of MO-CDs. However, the quantum yield of the composite drops to 9.3% due to the self-quenching effect caused by π–π stacking interaction in the solid state [10,12]. As depicted in Fig. 4c, the lamp constructed by MO-CDs/siloxane composite emits bright yellow light directly and the luminous region is mai

nly located in the range of 480–700 nm. In this emission range, the peak before 480 nm belongs to the UV chip light, and the one located at 480–700 nm belongs to the MO-CDs/siloxane composite. At the same time, the luminescence spectral position shows no noteworthy deviation compared with the solution. For another, as shown in Fig. 4d, the Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage (CIE) coordinate of this device is (0.31, 0.40). Accordingly, the color rendering index (CRI) and correlated color temperature (CCT) are 72.3 and 6279 K, respectively. On the other hand, compared with traditional phosphors and quantum dots made from rare earth elements, this device based on MO-CDs can shorten the preparation process, and reduce the production costs to obtain relatively cheap LED. These results are hard evidence of the good luminescence properties of MO-CDs in LED devices, which were promising to improve the color design in green lighting applications.

Fluorescent nanofiber has promising applications in fingerprint recognition, anti-counterfeiting, and dazzling textiles [37]. Electrospinning technology has proven to be the most direct and effective route to fabricating nonwoven nanofiber materials [38]. Therefore, we incorporate the fluorescent MO-CDs with electrospinning to prepare luminescent nanofibers (Fig. S1). As shown in Fig S3 and Fig. 5, the PAN nanofibers exhibit a 3D-crosslinking morphology with a relatively rough surface and are evenly distributed in the fields. Furthermore, according to the size distribution from Fig. S4, the mean width of the PAN nanofibers is about 450 nm. In addition, elemental mapping images (Fig. 5 b-c) clearly show that the PAN nanofibers are composed of C and N two elements. As demonstrated in Fig. 6 and Fig. S3, the morphology, roughness, and width of the glistening electrospinning are consistent with those of PAN nanofibers. As illustrated in Fig. 5f, the nanofibers-based MO-CDs emit bright yellow light at the excitation of 430 nm. Because of the low contrast and small size, it is too hard to directly observe the MO-CDs by TEM in the luminescent nanofibers. Nonetheless, the sodium, oxygen, and sulfur elements from MO-CDs are definite in the interior of the luminescent nanofibers based on the elemental mapping images (Fig. 6 d-f). On the other hand, we can see that the CDs are evenly distributed without agglomeration, which is conducive to large-scale production and anti-counterfeiting applications in the textile field.

In this study, yellow emission fluorescence MO-CDs were successfully prepared through a one-step hydrothermal method using the single precursor of Methyl Orange as the C source. Compared to the existing methods, our strategy saved the time of synthesizing and processing samples. Furthermore, the synthesized MO-CDs have excellent optical properties. In contrast with the conventional phosphors prepared from rare earth elements, the MO-CDs LED device not only reduces the cost but also simplifies the process of phosphors. On the other hand, the fluorescent nanofibers based MO-CDs will have mass production in the textile field and advanced anti-counterfeiting for practical applications. In summary, this study shows that MO-CDs will pave a new path to exploring the potential applications of CDs.

Yawen Zhou: Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Shuai Zhang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Shanggeng Li: Methodology, Investigation. Jing Li: Methodology, Formal analysis. Ning Li: Investigation, Funding acquisition. Lin Zhang: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51803198), Applied Basic Research Program of Sichuan Province (no. 2019YJ003), and State Key Laboratory of Environment-friendly Energy Materials (no. 19kfhg01). The authors are grateful to eceshi (www.eceshi.com) for the TEM measurements.
